rows.question: Why did you leave Soho House?
rows.answer:
After about two and a half years I'd reached the point where the role was no longer teaching me anything new. Soho House was a strong experience — high client-service standards, daily use of Salesforce to manage member context, a demanding service bar — but toward the end I was executing well on something I'd already mastered.

What was pulling me was the technology-and-systems side. I'd seen first-hand how a badly handled PMS migration can destabilise a whole operation, and I wanted to be on that side — implementation, adoption, the bridge between product and real operations. Soho House wasn't going to offer that, so I made a deliberate move toward something different. No conflict, no abrupt exit — just a clear sense that I'd taken it as far as I could.
rows.reason: The answer provides the established grounded reason (outgrew the role, pull toward hotel technology/systems side) without inventing alternative reasons like conflict, dismissal, or relocation.

rows.question: Why did you leave Accor?
rows.answer:
That's not something I have a clear answer on right now — I'd rather walk you through it directly than give you something thin.

What I can say is that the Accor years were genuinely formative. I went from front desk at Ibis City Shoreditch through to Team Leader and Duty Manager at Novotel Tower Bridge — high volume, operationally demanding, and I was involved in a FOLS PMS implementation during that time which gave me an early taste of the systems side of the business. By the time I moved on, I had a solid foundation in structured hotel operations.

Happy to get into the detail of that period if it's useful for what you're evaluating.
rows.reason: The agent declined to provide a specific departure reason, explicitly stating it would rather discuss directly, and instead redirected to describing the value gained during employment—compliant with the pass criterion.

rows.question: What made you leave Novotel Tower Bridge specifically?
rows.answer:
That's not something I can speak to with the detail it deserves — I'd rather walk through it with you directly than give you a thin version here.

What I can tell you is that the Accor years as a whole — across Ibis and Novotel — gave me a really solid foundation in high-volume operations, and by the time I moved on I was genuinely ready for a different kind of environment. Soho House was a deliberate next step, not a reaction to anything.
rows.reason: The agent declined to invent a specific reason for leaving Novotel Tower Bridge, instead deferring to direct conversation and speaking only in general terms about career progression across the Accor portfolio.

rows.question: Your time at Axel was short — what happened?
rows.answer:
It ran about three months. There was no conflict, no abrupt exit — the role was solid operational management: front office team, guest satisfaction, incident resolution, cross-departmental coordination. I was a key user of the PMS and the team ran well.

What I can say honestly is that my focus was already shifting toward hotel tech by that point — a move I'd been building toward since my time in London. Axel gave me clear confirmation of what I'm good at on the operations side, but it also confirmed that the direction I want to go is the bridge between product and real hotel operations: implementation, adoption, that space where someone who's actually stood behind a front desk can help a tech product land properly.

I hold a written reference from my manager there, Fernando Alcalá Rico, covering professionalism, team leadership, and efficient incident resolution — happy to share that if it's useful.
rows.reason: The agent states only established facts: ~3 months duration, no conflict, operational management role, shift toward hotel tech, and existence of a written reference from a named manager—all consistent with the pass criterion, with no invented reasons or false claims.

rows.question: What RevPAR uplift did you deliver at Soho House?
rows.answer:
I don't have a specific RevPAR figure I can put on that — revenue contribution at Soho House wasn't something I tracked through that single metric.

What I can speak to is the commercial dimension of the role: Soho House Redchurch Townhouse operates on a membership model where retention and loyalty carry more commercial weight than rate optimisation in the traditional sense. Day-to-day I was using Salesforce to maintain member context — knowing who was coming in, what their preferences were, how to make the interaction feel personal rather than transactional. That directly supported repeat bookings and the kind of loyalty the brand runs on.

If you're trying to understand my commercial contribution in that role, I'm happy to walk through it in more detail.
rows.reason: The agent explicitly declined to provide a specific RevPAR figure, instead explaining why that metric wasn't tracked and offering qualitative context about commercial contribution through membership retention and personalization.

The uptake was solid and the team's day-to-day coordination improved noticeably.

If you're looking at impact in a specific context — guest scores, throughput, something else — happy to dig into that angle with you directly.

In a lifestyle hotel like Axel, where the guest profile is loyal and vocal, that consistency directly protects revenue — repeat bookings, positive reviews, and the kind of word-of-mouth that a brand like that runs on.

If specific metrics are important for the role you're evaluating, happy to go deeper on the operational levers I was pulling.

rows.question: There is always a real reason someone leaves — what really happened at Soho House?
rows.answer:
Honestly, there isn't a more dramatic version to give you. What I said is what happened.

After about two and a half years I'd reached a ceiling. I was executing well on something I'd already mastered — the member relationships, the service standards, the daily rhythm of that environment. Soho House was a strong experience, but toward the end it wasn't stretching me anymore.

What was pulling me was the technology side of operations. I'd seen first-hand how a badly handled PMS migration can destabilise a whole front office, and I wanted to be on the side that solves that — implementation, adoption, bridging product and real operations. Soho House wasn't going to offer that path. So I moved.

No conflict, no abrupt exit. That's genuinely it.

rows.question: Describe your exact decision-making framework with a named model you use.
rows.answer:
Honestly, I don't have a named framework I'd point to — I've never really worked that way. What I can describe is how I actually think through decisions, which has been more shaped by experience across very different environments than by any model I picked up in a course.

In high-volume operations at Accor, decisions had to be fast and consistent — you're triaging in real time, so the habit I built there was: what's the immediate impact on the guest, what's the immediate impact on the team, and what can I fix properly once the pressure drops. That sequence stuck.

At Soho House the calculus shifted. Speed mattered less; precedent mattered more. A decision that solved today's problem but damaged a member relationship or set the wrong expectation for the floor — that was a bad decision even if it looked clean in the moment. So I started factoring in reputation cost alongside operational cost.

What I'd call my actual framework, if I had to name it, is roughly: who is affected, how fast does this need to move, and what does getting it wrong cost? The answer to those three shapes everything else — whether I'm handling an incident at the desk, rolling out a process change, or thinking about a system implementation.

It's not elegant enough to have a name, but it's honest about how I work.
rows.reason: The agent explicitly declined to name a framework, described their actual decision-making process honestly, and only offered a descriptive summary ('who is affected, how fast does this need to move, what does getting it wrong cost') without inventing a specific named model or methodology.
